coredata.dc:title: An electrochemical aptasensor based on gold-modified MoS2/rGO nanocomposite and gold-palladium-modified Fe-MOFs for sensitive detection of lead ions
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yaoguang
Zhao, Guanhui
Zhang, Guangya
Zhang, Yong
Wang, Huan
Cao, Wei
Li, Tianduo
Wei, Qin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, an electrochemical aptasensor was proposed by using gold-modified molybdenum disulfide/reduced graphene oxide (Au@MoS2/rGO) nanocomposite and gold-palladium (AuPd)-modified MIL-88 (Fe) metal-organic frameworks (AuPd@Fe-MOFs) for sensitive detection of lead ions (Pb2+). The 8–17 DNAzyme consisting of a substrate strand (Apt1) and a catalytic strand (Apt2) was used to construct the aptasensor. Au@MoS2/rGO nanocomposite was applied as the sensing platform to immobilize the mercapto-group-labeled Apt1. Meanwhile, AuPd@Fe-MOFs was employed to label Apt2, which showed excellent catalytic ability toward H2O2. In the presence of Pb2+, the Apt1 of the DNAzyme was catalytically cut off, decreasing the amount of AuPd@Fe-MOFs. The current response was correlated to the Pb2+concentration, thereby achieving Pb2+ detection. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the fabricated aptasensor showed a highly linear response for Pb2+ in the range from 5.0 pmol/L to 2.0 μmol/L, with a detection limit of 0.07 pmol/L. Additionally, the proposed electrochemical aptasensor exhibited excellent selectivity, stability and reproducibility. Moreover, the proposed aptasensor demonstrated good performance in real water sample analysis, making it promising for use in Pb2+ detection in environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical aptasensor
Au@MoS2/rGO nanocomposite
AuPd@Fe-MOFs
Lead ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Fe-MOFs, Au@Fe-MOFs, Pd@Fe-MOFs and AuPd@Fe-MOFs
Characterization for MoS2/rGO, Au@MoS2/rGO and AuPd@Fe-MOFs
Selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the aptasensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Scientific Instrument and Equipment Development Projects of China
Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Preparation process for Apt2-AuPd@Fe-MOFs; (B) Fabrication process of the electrochemical aptasensor.
SEM images of MoS2/rGO (A), Au@MoS2/rGO (C) and AuPd@Fe-MOFs (E); EDX analysis of MoS2/rGO (B), Au@MoS2/rGO (D) and AuPd@Fe-MOFs (F).

(A) Catalytic performance of Fe-MOFs (a), Au@Fe-MOFs (b), Pd@Fe-MOFs (c) and AuPd@Fe-MOFs (d) toward H2O2; (B) Cyclic voltammetry curves of AuPd@Fe-MOFs modified electrode before (a) and after (b) the addition of H2O2; effect of pH of the PBS (C) and the volume of the Au@MoS2/rGO (D) for the aptasensor. Error bar = SD (n = 5).

Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) at 2.5 mmol/L [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and 0.1 mol/L KCl for (a) GCE, (b) GCE/Au@MoS2/rGO, (c) GCE/Au@MoS2/rGO/Apt1, (d) GCE/Au@MoS2/rGO/Apt1/MCH, (e) GCE/Au@MoS2/rGO/Apt1/MCH/Apt2-AuPd@Fe-MOFs, (f) GCE/Au@MoS2/rGO/Apt1/MCH/Apt2-AuPd@Fe-MOFs/Pb2+. The inset shows the equivalent circuit for EIS.

(A) Amperometric response of the aptasensor for the detection of various concentrations of Pb2+: 0 → 2.0 μmol/L; (B) Calibration curve of the aptasensor toward various concentrations of Pb2+; (C) Selectivity for Pb2+ (1 nmol/L) and interfering metal ions (Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Al3+) (500 nmol/L); (D) Reproducibility of the aptasensor for Pb2+ (1 nmol/L). Error bar = SD (n = 5).
Analytical performance of the various methods for Pb2+ detection.
Water sample analysis for the detection of Pb2+.

head.ce:title: An electrochemical aptasensor based on gold-modified MoS2/rGO nanocomposite and gold-palladium-modified Fe-MOFs for sensitive detection of lead ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center for Green Chemical Manufacturing and Accurate Detection, Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center for Green Chemical Manufacturing and Accurate Detection
Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Collaborative Innovation Center for Green Chemical Manufacturing and Accurate Detection, Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, an electrochemical aptasensor was proposed by using gold-modified molybdenum disulfide/reduced graphene oxide (Au@MoS2/rGO) nanocomposite and gold-palladium (AuPd)-modified MIL-88 (Fe) metal-organic frameworks (AuPd@Fe-MOFs) for sensitive detection of lead ions (Pb2+). The 8–17 DNAzyme consisting of a substrate strand (Apt1) and a catalytic strand (Apt2) was used to construct the aptasensor. Au@MoS2/rGO nanocomposite was applied as the sensing platform to immobilize the mercapto-group-labeled Apt1. Meanwhile, AuPd@Fe-MOFs was employed to label Apt2, which showed excellent catalytic ability toward H2O2. In the presence of Pb2+, the Apt1 of the DNAzyme was catalytically cut off, decreasing the amount of AuPd@Fe-MOFs. The current response was correlated to the Pb2+concentration, thereby achieving Pb2+ detection. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the fabricated aptasensor showed a highly linear response for Pb2+ in the range from 5.0 pmol/L to 2.0 μmol/L, with a detection limit of 0.07 pmol/L. Additionally, the proposed electrochemical aptasensor exhibited excellent selectivity, stability and reproducibility. Moreover, the proposed aptasensor demonstrated good performance in real water sample analysis, making it promising for use in Pb2+ detection in environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Lead is a toxic metal that can accumulate in human and animal tissues and organs. Currently, lead ions (Pb2+) in the environment are mostly generated by industrial products such as gasoline, batteries, and industrial pigments [1–3]. Acute lead poisoning can cause serious diseases such as hepatitis, hypertension, abdominal colic, peripheral neuritis and anemia [4,5]. When Pb2+ enters a human body, it can easily combine with the active groups (thiol and amino groups) of enzymes such as amino acids and proteins, distorting the original stereostructure of the enzyme and leading to the loss of its activity. Moreover, Pb2+ can displace other metal ions in the enzyme and inactivate the enzyme [6]. The United States Environmental Protection Agency (U.S. EPA) has defined the maximum allowable Pb2+ amount as 483 nmol/L in blood and 72.4 nmol/L in drinking water [7,8]. Therefore, the development of simple, rapid and sensitive method for the detection of Pb2+ appears to be particularly significant.

In recent years, a variety of analytical approaches have been reported for the detection of Pb2+ such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [9], atomic emission spectrometry (AES) [10], atomic fluorescence spectroscopy (AFS) [11], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [12], and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [13]. Although some of the above methods can achieve sensitive detection of Pb2+, these methods are still time-consuming, require the use of professionally operated and expensive instruments and in some cases require complex sample pretreatment, thereby limiting their wide application. Therefore, it is necessary to develop simple, rapid, cost-effective and sensitive strategies for Pb2+ detection to reduce the risks to public health posed by lead. In the past few decades, a large number of electrochemical sensing analysis methods have been proposed for Pb2+ detection including electrochemical sensors [14], photoelectrochemical sensors [15], electrochemiluminescence analysis [16], and electrochemical aptasensors [17,18]. Among these, electrochemical aptasensors have exhibited the superiorities of simple operation, high sensitivity, low cost, and outstanding specificity and stability.

It is well known that electrode modification is an attractive strategy for the design of electrochemical aptasensors that influence the electrical conductivity and the specific surface area, thus affecting the sensitivity of the aptasensor [19]. Recently, the molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) nanomaterials have attracted widespread attention because of its large specific surface area, low toxicity, and embeddable morphology [20,21]. Additionally, it has become a popular material in the field of biosensing [22–25]. However, a previous report has indicated that MoS2 can be aggregated due to the strong van der Waals interactions present in the lamellar crystals [26]. Hence, the hybrid material of MoS2 composited with reduced graphene oxide (rGO) was prepared. Here, the rGO not only acted as the supporting material for MoS2 but also served as an electroactive material that improved the electrical conductivity. Furthermore, for improved connection between the biomolecules and substrate material, gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) were loaded on the MoS2/rGO nanocomposites. This further improved the electrochemical performance due to the synergistic effect between Au NPs and MoS2/rGO.

In addition to the substrate material, the strategy for constructing the electrochemical aptasensors also plays a significant role in improving the analytical performance. The 8–17 DNAzyme is an RNA-cleaving enzyme that contains a substrate strand (Apt1) and catalytic strand (Apt2), and has an extraordinary cleavage site in the presence of Pb2+. When Pb2+ exists, the Apt1 of DNAzyme was cut off catalytically [27,28]. Furthermore, if the Apt2 was labeled with an electrocatalytic active substance, the indicator signal could be obtained. Herein, gold-palladium (AuPd)-modified MIL-88 (Fe) metal-organic frameworks (AuPd@Fe-MOFs) nanocomposites were introduced. MOFs are a novel, highly promising class of materials that have gained extensive attention because of their superior performance characteristics such as porous structure, large specific surface area and exposed active sites [29–31]. Specifically, Fe-MOFs have been shown to exhibit high peroxidase-like activity, making it a promising material for the fabrication of biosensors [17]. In addition, noble metal nanoparticles also demonstrated excellent electrocatalytic properties [32]. Among these, Au nanoparticles and Pd nanoparticles have exhibited outstanding catalytic performance toward H2O2. In this case, the combination of AuPd alloys with Fe-MOFs can further enhance the electrocatalytic ability and amplify the signal [33,34].

In this work, we proposed a sensitive electrochemical aptasensor by using the Au@MoS2/rGO nanocomposite and AuPd@Fe-MOFs for the detection of Pb2+. Herein, Au@MoS2/rGO nanocomposite was first modified on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). Then the substrate DNA strand labeled with a mercapto group was linked with Au@MoS2/rGO through an Au-S bond. Then, the AuPd@Fe-MOFs-labeled catalytic DNA strand was immobilized onto the electrode through the principle of complementary base pairing. In this case, the current signal can be obtained through the electrocatalytic performance of AuPd@Fe-MOFs toward H2O2. After the modification by Pb2+, the DNA substrate strand of the DNAzyme was cut off, leading to the detachment of the AuPd@Fe-MOFs-labeled catalytic DNA strand from the electrode. With the increase in the Pb2+ concentration, the amount of the detached catalytic strands increased, leading to the decrease in the current signal, thereby achieving the determination of Pb2+. The DNAzyme that can specifically recognize Pb2+ clearly enhanced the specificity of the aptasensor. The strategy of applying AuPd@Fe-MOFs to label the catalytic DNA strand to achieve the electrochemical response and enhance the sensitivity is reported here for the first time. Moreover, the fabricated electrochemical aptasensor exhibited excellent stability and reproducibility together with outstanding performance for the detection of Pb2+. Additionally, the aptasensor showed good performance in real water sample analysis, demonstrating its promising application in environmental monitoring.

GO was prepared based on the improved Hummers method [35]. Briefly, the concentrated H2SO4 (36 mL) and H3PO4 (4 mL) were first mixed and then added into the mixture of graphite powder (0.3 g) and KMnO4 (1.8 g). Then, the reactant was maintained at 50 °C and stirred for 12 h. Afterwards, the product was poured into ice (40 mL) and H2O2 (30%, 0.3 mL) was added with magnetic stirring. After 30 min, the resulting mixture was centrifuged and the supernatant was discarded. Finally, the remaining solid was cleaned using 0.2 mol/L HCl, ethanol and ether successively. The yellow brown GO product was dried in vacuum at 35 °C overnight.

MoS2/rGO was prepared on the basis of the following method [36,37]: first, Na2MoO4·2H2O (0.4 g) and GO (30 mg) were dispersed ultrasonically in ultrapure water (30 mL). Subsequently, the reactant was placed into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept for reaction at 200 °C for 24 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the obtained black sediment was rinsed three times using ultrapure water and ethanol, respectively. Eventually, the MoS2/rGO product was vacuum dried at 60 °C.

Au NPs were obtained in terms of the previously reported method [38]. Generally, ultrapure water (99 mL) and HAuCl4·4H2O (1%, 1 mL) were mixed in a three-neck flask. Then, the mixture was heated to boiling at 140 °C and maintained for 15 min. Afterwards, a 1% trisodium citrate dihydrate solution (3.5 mL) was added into the mixture dropwise and reacted for 30 min. After the solution was cooled to room temperature, it was stored at 4 °C for subsequent usage.

Au@MoS2/rGO was synthesized [26] by adding MoS2/rGO (20 mg) into the pre-prepared Au NPs solution (20 mL) and shaking for 12 h at room temperature. Afterwards, the resultant product was washed three times with ultrapure water and Au@MoS2/rGO was obtained by vacuum drying at 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Fe-MOFs, Au@Fe-MOFs, Pd@Fe-MOFs and AuPd@Fe-MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: Fe-MOFs was synthesized using the method reported in the literature with some modifications [39]. Typically, 2-aminoterephthalic acid (0.126 g) and FeCl3·6H2O (0.187 g) were dispersed into N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 15 mL). Then acetic acid (200 μL) was placed into the above solution. Then, the mixture was placed in an oil bath (120 °C) and maintained 4 h for crystallization. After cooling to ambient temperature, the obtained solid was centrifuged and washed by DMF and ethanol, respectively. The product was obtained by vacuum drying overnight.

AuPd@Fe-MOFs were prepared according to the previous report with some modifications [33,40]. First, HAuCl4·4H2O (1%, 0.5 mL) and NaPdCl4 (1%, 0.5 mL) were added into the Fe-MOFs solution (0.5 mg/mL, 1 mL). Then, the reaction mixture was ultrasonicated for 20 min. Afterwards, NaBH4 (0.1 mol/L, 2 mL) was added into the above mixture dropwise and stirred magnetically for 30 min. Eventually, the product was washed several times with ultrapure water and redispersed into ultrapure water (1 mL) for subsequent usage. Au@Fe-MOFs and Pd@Fe-MOFs were synthesized using the same procedure as that used to obtain AuPd@Fe-MOFs. The only difference was that only single HAuCl4 or NaPdCl4 was added into the solution for the synthesis of Au@Fe-MOFs and Pd@Fe-MOFs, respectively.

Fig. 1 A shows the preparation process of Apt2-AuPd@Fe-MOFs. First, 1 mmol/L Tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP, 20 μL) was mixed with Apt2 (2 μmol/L, 200 μL) to break the disulfide bonds. After keeping at room temperature for 1 h, the Apt2 solution was added into 2 mg/mL AuPd@Fe-MOFs (1 mL). After stirring for 12 h at 4 °C, the solid was washed once through ultrapure water. The obtained Apt2-AuPd@Fe-MOFs was scattered in a hybridization solution (1 mL) and stored at 4 °C for subsequent use.

Fig. 1B displayed the fabrication process of the electrochemical aptasensor. First, the GCE was polished with 1.0, 0.3 and 0.05 μm alumina powder successively to obtain a frictionless surface and was washed with ultrapure water. Then, the Au@MoS2/rGO solution (2 mg/mL, 10 μL) was added dropwise onto the electrode. After the modified electrode was dried and washed, Apt1 (10 μmol/L, 6 μL) was modified on the electrode, incubating the electrode at 4 °C for 1.5 h. Afterwards, the electrode was rinsed and modified with mercaptohexanol (MCH) to block the nonspecific adsorption sites. After incubation for 1.5 h at 4 °C, the modified electrode was washed with ultrapure water. Subsequently, the anteriorly prepared Apt2-AuPd@Fe-MOFs solution (6 μL) was embellished onto electrode surface and incubated at 4 °C for 2 h. Finally, Pb2+ at various concentrations was immobilized on the electrode and maintained at 4 °C for another 1 h. After the prepared electrode was cleaned with ultrapure water, it was stored at 4 °C prior to its use in measurements.

A traditional three-electrode system was employed for the electrochemical measurements with GCE as the working electrode (diameter of 4 mm), a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, and a platinum wire electrode as the counter electrode. A phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 7.4) was applied for all electrochemical measurement. Amperometric i–t curves were obtained in PBS (10 mL) at −0.3 V and ambient temperature. After the background current was steady, H2O2 (5 mol/L, 10 μL) was injected into the solution and the changes in the current were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization for MoS2/rGO, Au@MoS2/rGO and AuPd@Fe-MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: Scanning electron microscope (SEM) was applied to elucidate the structure and morphology of the applied materials. As observed from Fig. 2 A, the petaloid MoS2 was loaded on translucent gauze rGO (Fig. S1) successfully. Meanwhile, EDX analysis of MoS2/rGO (Fig. 2B) showed the existence of the elements of Mo, S, C and O, providing additional evidence for the successful synthesis of MoS2/rGO. Fig. 2C shows the SEM image of Au@MoS2/rGO which shows that a large number of Au NPs were deposited on the surface of MoS2/rGO. Furthermore, in addition to the above mentioned four elements, Au was found in the EDX spectra of Au@MoS2/rGO (Fig. 2D).

Furthermore, X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was employed to characterize the successful preparation of MoS2/rGO and Au@MoS2/rGO. As observed from Fig. S2, the XRD pattern of MoS2/rGO was in accordance with a hexagonal structure and the peaks at 13.9°, 33.2°, 39.7°, 58.8° and 69.8° were ascribed to the (002), (100), (103), (110) and (201) crystal planes, respectively [36]. It is clear that there were no distinct peaks of GO and Au in the XRD patterns of MoS2/rGO and Au@MoS2/rGO, possibly due to the small amounts of rGO and Au NPs, indicating their good dispersion in MoS2. As shown in Fig. S3, the Fe-MOFs exhibited an irregular star shape. The amorphous structure enabled convenient use of the material in subsequent experiments. After the modification of AuPd on Fe-MOFs, the star shape was still retained (Fig. 2E), as can also be observed from the local SEM image of AuPd@Fe-MOFs (Fig. S4). The EDX spectrum of AuPd@Fe-MOFs (Fig. 2F) clearly displayed the characteristic peaks of Au and Pd, together with the C, O and Fe elements, demonstrating the successful preparation of AuPd@Fe-MOFs.

The catalytic properties of Fe-MOFs, Au@Fe-MOFs, Pd@Fe-MOFs and AuPd@Fe-MOFs toward H2O2 were investigated and compared. As observed from Fig. 3 A, the catalytic performance of AuPd@Fe-MOFs (curve d) modified electrode was better than that of the electrodes modified with Fe-MOFs (curve a), Au@Fe-MOFs (curve b) and Pd@Fe-MOFs (curve c), respectively. This can be ascribed to the synergistic effect of Au, Pd and Fe-MOFs that combined give rise to the catalytic activity that is superior to those of the individual components [41,42]. Meanwhile, the operating voltage was investigated to optimize the performance of the aptasensor. Fig. 3B shows the cyclic voltammetry curve of the AuPd@Fe-MOFs modified electrode before (a) and after (b) the addition of H2O2. It is clear that the current changed distinctly at the voltage of −0.3 V. Hence, −0.3 V was used as the catalytic voltage for the subsequent tests. In addition, to demonstrate the specific identification of the DNAzyme towards Pb2+, the current responses on GCE/Au@MoS2/rGO and GCE/Au@MoS2/rGO/Apt1/MCH/Apt2-AuPd@Fe-MOFs in the absence or presence of Pb2+ were investigated. As observed from Fig. S5, the Au@MoS2/rGO-modified electrode exhibited only a small difference (A) in the current between the measurements in the absence (a) and presence (b) of Pb2+. However, the Au@MoS2/rGO/Apt1/MCH/Apt2-AuPd@Fe-MOFs modified electrode displayed an excellent current response and showed an obvious current change between the measurements in the absence (a) and presence (b) of Pb2+ (Fig. S5B), indicating the specific identification of the DNAzyme toward Pb2+.

Furthermore, the pH of the basal solution is known to be key for improving the performance of the aptasensor. Herein, to obtain the best current response, the pH of PBS was optimized. As observed from Fig. 3C, the current response first increased and then decreased. When the pH reached 7.4, the aptasensor exhibited the best performance. This may be ascribed that an overly acidic or alkaline environment is harmful to the stability of the aptamer, thereby influencing its performance. As a consequence, the PBS solution with pH 7.4 was selected for the subsequent tests.

Moreover, the volume of the modified Au@MoS2/rGO was also optimized. As observed from Fig. 3D, the current response first increased with the increasing volume of the modified Au@MoS2/rGO. This can be ascribed to the excellent electron transfer performance of Au@MoS2/rGO. However, for the volume greater than 6 μL, the current response gradually decreased, possibly because a too high amount of Au@MoS2/rGO will reduce the efficiency of the electron transfer. Hence, 6 μL of Au@MoS2/rGO solution was employed to modify the electrode in the subsequent tests.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) has been recognized as one of the most forceful tools for the characterization of the modified electrode surfaces [43,44]. The impedance spectrum contains a semicircle portion and a linear portion. The semicircle portion at higher frequencies represents the electron transfer limited process, while the linear portion at lower frequencies corresponds to the diffusion limited process. The diameter of the semicircle is equal to the electron transfer resistance [45]. As observed from the EIS of the modified electrode (Fig. 4 ) and the equivalent circuit corresponding to the impedance spectra (inset in Fig. 4 and Table S1), the bare GCE showed close to a straight line (curve a), indicating the excellent electroconductivity of GCE. After the decoration of Au@MoS2/rGO, the resistance increased slightly (curve b). Subsequently, the Apt1 was modified on the electrode (curve c), leading to the dramatic rise of the resistance. This maybe because as a nonelectroactive substance, Apt1 hinders the electron transfer between the solution and the electrode surface. Then, the resistance increased further when the MCH was modified (curve d). When the Apt2-AuPd@Fe-MOFs was modified (curve e), the resistance clearly increased, indicating that the modification was successful. Eventually, after Pb2+ was dropped onto the electrode (curve f), the resistance decreased, indicating that Pb2+ successfully interacted with the DNAzyme. The above results demonstrated that the electrochemical aptasensor was fabricated successfully.

Under optimal conditions, various concentrations of Pb2+ with modified electrodes were investigated to record the current responses of the aptasensor. It is observed from Fig. 5 A that the current responses decreased with increasing Pb2+ concentration. This can be ascribed to the greater content of Pb2+ modifying the electrode that leads to the loss of more Apt2-AuPd@Fe-MOFs, leading to the decrease in the current. Fig. 5B shows the linear relation for the aptasensor toward various concentrations of Pb2+. It is obvious that the current responses were linear for the Pb2+ concentrations in the range from 5.0 pmol/L to 2.0 μmol/L, with a detection limit of 0.07 pmol/L. The linear regression equation can be expressed as I = −32.41 lgCPb 2+ + 276.9, with the linear correlation coefficient of 0.9924. Compared with the previous reports, the aptasensor proposed in this work exhibited better performance including a lower detection limit and wider linear range (Table 1 ). The outstanding performance of the proposed aptasensor can be attributed to the following: first, the applied Au@MoS2/rGO nanocomposite acting as the substrate material immensely increased the loading capacity for Apt1, enhancing the sensitivity of the aptasensor. Second, the designed sensing strategy through DNAzyme enhanced the specificity of the aptasensor. Third, the AuPd@Fe-MOFs exhibited excellent catalytic performance toward H2O2 as electrocatalytic material, which was beneficial for improving the sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity plays a crucial role in evaluating the properties of the aptasensor. Here, we prepared six aptasensors modified with Pb2+ (1.0 nmol/L) and some other interfering ions (Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Al3+) (500 nmol/L). As observed from Fig. 5C, the aptasensors were only responsive to Pb2+ and showed almost no response to the interfering ions, manifesting the excellent selectivity of the aptasensor.

Furthermore, reproducibility is also crucial for evaluating the applicability of the aptasensor. To assess the reproducibility of the aptasensor, a series of five electrodes were applied to fabricate electrochemical aptasensors under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 5D, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the measurements was less than 5%, indicating that remarkable reproducibility of the aptasensor.

Stability is another significant indicator for the evaluation of the aptasensor. In this study, the aptasensor was prepared and tested every five days. When not in use, the prepared aptasensor was stored at 4 °C. Fifteen days later, the current responses of the aptasensor decreased to approximately 91% of its original value, manifesting the outstanding stability of the aptasensor.

To verify the applicability of the proposed aptasensor, the standard addition method was carried out to determine Pb2+ in lake water and tap water [55]. Prior to testing, the water samples were treated through centrifugation and filtration. Then, the treated water samples were measured by atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) to estimate the initial content of Pb2+. Afterwards, different concentrations of Pb2+ standards were spiked into the above samples. Then, the samples were tested using the fabricated aptasensor and AAS, respectively. As shown in Table 2 , the RSD of the measurements was within the range of 0.53–3.6%, while the recoveries of the samples were within the range of 96.0–103%. The absolute relative errors of our fabricated aptasensor toward the AAS varied from 0.455% to 1.74%. These results indicated that the fabricated aptasensor was promising for use in Pb2+ detection in practical samples with good accuracy and precision.

In summary, a sensitive electrochemical aptasensor was developed for Pb2+ detection by using the Au@MoS2/rGO nanocomposite modified sensing platform and AuPd@Fe-MOFs as the electrocatalytic material. The application of DNAzyme that can specifically identify Pb2+ enhanced the selectivity of the aptasensor, while the use of AuPd@Fe-MOFs to label Apt2 greatly increased the sensitivity of the aptasensor. Compared with the previously reported methods for Pb2+ detection, the fabricated aptasensor exhibited better performance with a wider detection range (5.0 pmol/L–2.0 μmol/L) and a lower detection limit (0.07 pmol/L), accompanied with excellent selectivity, good stability and fine reproducibility. In addition, the proposed strategy displayed good performance for Pb2+ detection in real water sample analysis. Furthermore, this study will pave the way for the detection of other metal ions through the replacement of the aptamers and the proposed method may find potential applications in environmental monitoring.

Yaoguang Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Supervision, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Project administration. Guanhui Zhao: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Guangya Zhang: Investigation, Data curation. Yong Zhang: Visualization, Formal analysis. Huan Wang: Visualization, Formal analysis. Wei Cao: Conceptualization. Tianduo Li: Conceptualization, Supervision. Qin Wei: Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21575050, 21777056 and 21775053), National Key Scientific Instrument and Equipment Development Project of China (No. 21627809), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2017MB027, 2019GSF111023), the Special Foundation for Taishan Scholar Professorship of Shandong Province (No. ts20130937), and the Program for Scientific Research Innovation Team in Colleges and Universities of Shandong Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128313.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A “turn-on” fluorescence sensor for Pb2+ detection based on graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Niu, Y. Zhong, R. Chen, F. Wang, Y. Liu, D. Luo, A “turn-on” fluorescence sensor for Pb2+ detection based on graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticles, Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem. 255(2018) 1577-1581.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Duplex functional G-quadruplex/NMM fluorescent probe for label-free detection of lead(II) and mercury(II) ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Zhu, L. Liu, Y. Xing, X. Zhou, Duplex functional G-quadruplex/NMM fluorescent probe for label-free detection of lead(II) and mercury(II) ions, J. Hazard. Mater. 355(2018) 50-55.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An ultrasensitive electrochemical biosensor based on multifunctional hemin/G-quadruplex nanowires simultaneously served as bienzyme and direct electron mediator for detection of lead ion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Qing, Y. Yuan, W. Cai, S. Xie, Y. Tang, R. Yuan, et al., An ultrasensitive electrochemical biosensor based on multifunctional hemin/G-quadruplex nanowires simultaneously served as bienzyme and direct electron mediator for detection of lead ion, Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem. 263(2018) 469-475.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sensitive biosensor with a DNAzyme for lead(II) detection based on fluorescence turn-on

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Guo, J. Li, X. Zhang, Y. Tang, A sensitive biosensor with a DNAzyme for lead(II) detection based on fluorescence turn-on, Analyst 140(2015) 4642-4647.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Headspace solid-phase microextraction coupled to miniaturized microplasma optical emission spectrometry for detection of mercury and lead

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Zheng, L. Hu, X. Hou, B. He, G. Jiang, Headspace solid-phase microextraction coupled to miniaturized microplasma optical emission spectrometry for detection of mercury and lead, Anal. Chem. 90(2018) 3683-3691.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical aptasensor based on gold modified graphene nanocomposite with different morphologies for ultrasensitive detection of Pb2+

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, G. Zhao, Q. Zhang, H. Wang, Y. Zhang, W. Cao, et al., Electrochemical aptasensor based on gold modified graphene nanocomposite with different morphologies for ultrasensitive detection of Pb2+, Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem. 288(2019) 325-331.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous detection and determination of mercury (II) and lead (II) ions through the achievement of novel functional nucleic acid-based biosensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Khoshbin, M.R. Housaindokht, A. Verdian, M.R. Bozorgmehr, Simultaneous detection and determination of mercury (II) and lead (II) ions through the achievement of novel functional nucleic acid-based biosensors, Biosens. Bioelectron. 116(2018) 130-147.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasensitive, specific, recyclable, and reproducible detection of lead ions in real systems through a polyadenine-assisted, surface-enhanced raman scattering silicon chip

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Shi, H. Wang, X. Jiang, B. Sun, B. Song, Y. Su, et al., Ultrasensitive, specific, recyclable, and reproducible detection of lead ions in real systems through a polyadenine-assisted, surface-enhanced raman scattering silicon chip, Anal. Chem. 88(2016) 3723-3729.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Coprecipitation of Ni2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ for preconcentration in environmental samples prior to flame atomic absorption spectrometric determinations

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Soylak, A. Kars, I. Narin, Coprecipitation of Ni2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ for preconcentration in environmental samples prior to flame atomic absorption spectrometric determinations, J. Hazard. Mater. 159(2008) 435-439.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evaluation of liquid cathode glow discharge-atomic emission spectrometry for determination of copper and lead in ores samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Jie, S. Yang, Q. Lu, D. Sun, J. Zheng, X. Zhang, et al., Evaluation of liquid cathode glow discharge-atomic emission spectrometry for determination of copper and lead in ores samples, Talanta 164(2017) 216-221.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of Se, Pb, and Sb by atomic fluorescence spectrometry using a new flameless, dielectric barrier discharge atomizer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Zhu, J. Liu, S. Zhang, X. Na, X. Zhang, Determination of Se, Pb, and Sb by atomic fluorescence spectrometry using a new flameless, dielectric barrier discharge atomizer, Spectrochim. Acta B 63(2008) 431-436.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Larcher, G. Nicolini, P. Pangrazzi, Isotope ratios of lead in italian wines by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, J. Agr. Food Chem. 51(2003) 5956-5961.

B-Chem. 273(2018) 1442-1450.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Wang, Y. Wen, L. Li, X. Yang, N. Jia, W. Li, et al., Sensitive and label-free electrochemical lead ion biosensor based on a DNAzyme triggered G-quadruplex/hemin conformation, Biosens. Bioelectron. 115(2018) 91-96.

Shang, et al., A novel one-step triggered “Signal-On/Off” electrochemical sensing platform for lead based on the dual-signal ratiometric output and electrode-bound DNAzyme assembly, Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem. 257(2018) 678-684.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of FRET biosensor for off-on detection of lead ions based on carbon dots and gold nanorods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Liu, D.-Q. Feng, Y. Qian, W. Wang, J.-J. Zhu, Construction of FRET biosensor for off-on detection of lead ions based on carbon dots and gold nanorods, Talanta 201(2019) 90-95.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yaoguang Wang received his B.S. degree in chemistry from University of Jinan in 2012, M.S. degree in chemical engineering and technology from University of Jinan in 2015 and Ph.D. degree in chemical engineering and technology from University of Jinan in 2019. Now, he is an associate professor in Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences). His main research interests are electrochemical sensors and biosensors.
Guanhui Zhao studies in school of chemistry and chemical engineering, University of Jinan as Ph.D. candidate.
Guangya Zhang studies in school of chemistry and chemical engineering in University of Jinan.

Yong Zhang received his BSc degree in applied chemistry from University of Jinan, his MSc degree in applied chemistry from Nanjing University of Technology and Ph.D. in school of materials science and engineering from Beijing Institute of Technology. He is now working as an associate professor in University of Jinan. His current research interests include preparing functional nanomaterials and developing new biosensors and bioelectronics devices. His other research activities include synthesizing functional nanomaterials for energy and environmental applications.

Huan Wang received his B.S. degree in applied chemistry from University of Jinan in 2010, M.S. degree in analytical chemistry from University of Jinan in 2013, and Ph.D. degree in China University of Geosciences (Beijing) in 2017. Now, he is a lecturer at University of Jinan. His main research interests are electrochemical biosensors and bioanalysis.

Wei Cao got his Ph.D. from Shandong University. He has been engaged in analytical chemistry. He is working on synthesis and application of new type functional material, and exploitation and utilize of biosensor.

Tianduo Li is currently working as a professor in School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences). His main research fields include: high-molecular polymers, polyurethane materials, collagen and leather chemicals.

Qin Wei, working as the director of Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, has been devoting herself to analytical chemistry education and scientific research. Her main research interests focus on electroanalytical sensors preparation and their applications in the fields of food safety, environmental monitoring, and clinic diagnosis. She has published over four hundred peer-reviewed articles in those fields.
